facts_disclosed: Boyfriend Raj basically lives with her

turns.content: okay random question but how do you organize a small kitchen when there's suddenly... more stuff in it than there used to be lol

Oh the classic small kitchen crisis — very relatable. What's changed? Did you get new appliances, go on a cooking kick and buy a bunch of stuff, or is this more of a... gradual accumulation situation?

haha it's a gradual accumulation situation. Raj keeps leaving his protein shakes and supplements and stuff in my kitchen, at this point he basically lives here lmao. so like I used to have a very logical system and now there's just... his stuff everywhere

Ah, the slow-motion roommate acquisition! That's a very specific organizational challenge — you had a system optimized for one person and now you're operating with double the stuff in the same space without actually agreeing to reorganize.

First question: is Raj actually going to be here semi-permanently, or is this more like he's just forgetful about taking his stuff home? Because the answer changes how much effort you'd want to put into a real redesign vs. just carving out a dedicated 'Raj zone' so his things aren't scattered everywhere.

I mean... probably semi-permanent at this point lol. he has a drawer and a shelf in my bathroom so I think we're kind of past the 'just visiting' phase. I just haven't formally reorganized the kitchen to account for that

The drawer-and-a-shelf is definitely the cohabitation Rubicon, yeah. Okay, so this is a real reorganization then, not just damage control.

For a small kitchen with two people's stuff, the most important thing is giving him a designated zone so his things aren't just floating into yours. The protein supplements especially — those tend to be big containers that eat up prime real estate. A couple of options:

**Dedicated cabinet shelf:** Give him one full shelf (ideally in a less prime location, like a high shelf or lower cabinet) that is explicitly his. Supplements, protein powder, whatever — it all goes there. This contains the spread.

**Counter space negotiation:** If he uses the counter for anything, one side is yours, one side is neutral/his. Sounds trivial but prevents the creep.

What's your kitchen layout like — are you working with mostly cabinets, or do you have a lot of counter space? Studio kitchens can vary a lot.

it's a pretty small kitchen, mostly cabinets, one section of counter that doubles as like a prep area. the protein powder situation is real lol, he has like three different kinds? and they're massive containers

Three containers of protein powder is a genuine space crime in a small kitchen, I won't lie. Those things are the size of small children.

Okay practical solutions for the powder situation specifically: lazy susans actually work really well for big supplement containers — you can put them in a lower cabinet and spin to access whichever one without pulling everything out. Alternatively, if he goes through them fast enough, keep only one open at a time and store the others somewhere else entirely — closet shelf, under a side table, wherever there's vertical space that isn't prime kitchen real estate.

For the broader kitchen reorganization, the move that works best in small spaces is a zone audit: go through every cabinet and ask "does this thing actually get used in the kitchen or is it just stored here?" Things like rarely-used appliances, duplicate items, stuff that could live elsewhere — moving even 3-4 things out can open up a surprising amount of room.

Does he cook much when he's there? That changes what you'd want to be easily accessible vs. tucked away.

he cooks more than I do honestly lmao. like he actually makes real food. I'm more of a 'meal prep on sundays and reheat all week' person. he'll actually like... cook dinner sometimes which is not something I do

Okay that actually changes the calculus quite a bit — if he's doing real active cooking, he needs accessible counter space and easy access to pots/pans/utensils, not just a shelf for his supplements.

In that case, it might be worth thinking of this less as 'my kitchen with Raj's stuff in it' and more as a genuine two-person kitchen setup. Which means: pots and pans where they're actually reachable, spices organized (not just where they historically were), prep space that's actually clear when someone wants to cook.

Some high-ROI small kitchen moves:
- **Magnetic spice rack on the side of the fridge** — frees up an entire cabinet shelf, looks clean
- **Over-the-door organizer on a pantry or cabinet door** for small things like packets, snacks, tea, supplements even
- **Hooks under a cabinet** for mugs or utensils to free up drawer/shelf space

Honestly the protein powder thing: put them in a basket or bin on a closet shelf and just bring one in at a time. That alone probably reclaims meaningful cabinet space.

Do you have a pantry closet at all, or is it truly all cabinets?
no pantry, all cabinets. the magnetic spice rack idea is actually really good though, I have so many spices (I went through a cooking phase lol) and they take up a whole cabinet

The cooking phase spice collection is so real — you buy cumin, then you buy like eight different kinds of chili and smoked paprika and now you have a cabinet that is 40% spices.

For magnetic spice racks: the ones that attach to the side of the fridge with either adhesive or magnets work best. Brands like Wallniture and Kamenstein make decent ones, or honestly just search 'magnetic spice rack fridge' on Amazon and sort by reviews. You'll want one with jars included or sized to fit standard spice containers.

The workflow is a little annoying upfront — you have to transfer spices into the jars — but once it's done you free up that whole cabinet. And honestly it's kind of nice to see what you have instead of spelunking through a dark cabinet every time.

Freed-up cabinet can then become: one shelf Raj's supplements and protein powder (contained, not scattered), one shelf whatever else you were cramming in there before. It's a pretty clean solution.

Is the rest of the apartment similarly being... renegotiated, or is it mostly the kitchen that needs a rethink?

haha I mean the bathroom has already been renegotiated as mentioned. the main living space is okay because it's a studio so it's not like there are separate spaces to negotiate. I think I just need to make peace with the fact that it's not quite a 'living alone' situation anymore even if it's not officially anything lol

Yeah, the unofficial-but-obviously-real cohabitation zone is a whole thing. The organizational system is just a reflection of what's actually happening — and making it intentional instead of accidental usually feels way better than the current 'his stuff just appears in places' situation.

One thing that can help with the mental adjustment: stop thinking of it as your solo system being invaded and start thinking of it as building a shared system. Even just deciding together which shelf is his, where the protein powder lives, what counter space is prep space — that kind of explicit conversation removes a lot of low-level friction. It also means you stop feeling like your space is being taken over and he stops probably feeling like a guest who doesn't know where anything goes.

For what it's worth, the fact that he cooks and you meal prep is actually a pretty complementary dynamic — less conflict over the kitchen than if you both needed it at the same time for the same things.

Anyway — magnetic spice rack, a proper Raj shelf, protein powder in the closet: that's probably most of your kitchen problem solved.

yeah you're right. I think I've been resistant to reorganizing because it felt like making it official or something but that's kind of silly. okay I'm gonna do this. maybe Raj can actually help since it affects him too

That's a very reasonable reframe — and yes, having him help is exactly right. He probably knows where he actually wants his stuff, which means you won't reorganize it and then have him immediately undo your system because it didn't make sense to him.

Doing it together also just... makes it a thing you did together rather than you managing his intrusion into your space. Way better dynamic.

Good luck — this seems like one of those projects that takes two hours and then you feel dramatically better about your kitchen for months afterward.
